message:
chore(beads): update issue tracker with checkpoint and perf SLO entries

Sync .beads/issues.jsonl to reflect new issues and status changes
related to gradient checkpointing, performance SLO framework, and
property-based test coverage work.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -5,9 +5,10 @@
 {"id":"frankentorch-18a","title":"Align proposed architecture crate map with current workspace layers","description":"Update PROPOSED_ARCHITECTURE.md so its crate map and runtime flow mention the in-tree ft-nn, ft-optim, and ft-data layers instead of stopping at ft-api/ft-conformance.","notes":"Updated the proposed architecture doc so the crate map includes ft-nn, ft-optim, and ft-data, and the runtime flow now acknowledges those higher-level layers before conformance/durability.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-03-28T05:09:02.557560325Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-28T05:09:21.467027815Z","closed_at":"2026-03-28T05:09:21.466400527Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-1le","title":"Investigate persistent beads SQLite integrity warning after clean rebuild","description":"## Context
After a full non-destructive rebuild of `.beads/beads.db` from canonical `issues.jsonl`, `br doctor --json` still reports:

`sqlite.integrity_check: database disk image is malformed: page 17 is never used`

This persists even on the freshly rebuilt DB, while all operational checks are otherwise healthy (`br ready --json` is empty, sync metadata matches, blocked cache is not stale).

## Goal
Determine whether this warning reflects:
1. a genuine low-level SQLite file/layout problem,
2. expected frankensqlite behavior that `br doctor` should not flag as suspicious, or
3. a beads/br bug in the integrity-check path.

## Acceptance
- Reproduce the warning on the current canonical DB
- Compare against a freshly rebuilt temp DB
- Gather enough evidence to classify the issue as workspace-local vs tool-level
- If it is tool-level or not safely fixable from this repo, document the finding clearly and close or hand off with evidence","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-03T13:56:40.262095265Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T14:00:06.096361407Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T14:00:06.096186135Z","close_reason":"Reproduced the integrity warning on canonical and rebuilt DBs, proved a plain SQLite VACUUM clears it, then observed br doctor --repair reintroduce the warning on a different page number while keeping logical metadata healthy; this classifies the issue as a tool-level beads/frankensqlite write-path problem rather than repo-local issue-data corruption","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-1s2","title":"Add F.pad reflect/replicate/circular modes","description":"Extend tensor_pad to support reflect, replicate, circular padding modes. Currently only constant padding. Essential for vision preprocessing.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-02T19:49:10.441649598Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-02T19:53:41.804652781Z","closed_at":"2026-04-02T19:53:41.804379905Z","close_reason":"Implemented tensor_pad_mode with reflect, replicate, circular modes. 6 tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankentorch-2er","title":"Add custom autograd Function API (torch.autograd.Function equivalent)","description":"Users cannot currently define custom backward functions. This is arguably the single most impactful missing feature for real-world use — without it, any layer whose backward isn't built into FrankenTorch's op set is impossible to implement.

PyTorch's torch.autograd.Function API allows users to:
1. Define a custom forward() that registers itself on the autograd tape
2. Define a custom backward() that computes gradients for the custom op
3. Save tensors for backward via ctx.save_for_backward()

Implementation plan:
1. Add a CustomFunction trait in ft-autograd with forward/backward methods
2. Add a TensorNodeOp::Custom variant to the tensor tape
3. Add session.apply_custom_function() to ft-api that invokes the user's forward, registers the op on the tape, and stores the backward closure
4. During backward traversal, call the user's backward function when encountering Custom nodes
5. Handle save_for_backward by capturing TensorNodeIds in the closure context

Key design decisions:
- The backward closure must receive upstream gradient and return downstream gradients
- Must respect no_grad context (custom forward inside no_grad should not record on tape)
- Must work with the existing deterministic backward scheduler
- Error handling: user backward functions should return Result, not panic

Tests:
- Unit test: custom ReLU equivalent (forward: max(0,x), backward: 1 if x>0 else 0)
- Unit test: custom function with multiple inputs/outputs
- Unit test: custom function inside no_grad context (should not record)
- Unit test: custom function backward produces correct gradients (numerical gradient check)
- E2E test: train a model with a custom layer and verify loss decreases
- Edge case: nested custom functions
- Edge case: custom function that saves tensors for backward","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:16:46.006446323Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:23:12.307106481Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankentorch-2er","title":"Add custom autograd Function API (torch.autograd.Function equivalent)","description":"Users cannot currently define custom backward functions. This is arguably the single most impactful missing feature for real-world use — without it, any layer whose backward isn't built into FrankenTorch's op set is impossible to implement.

PyTorch's torch.autograd.Function API allows users to:
1. Define a custom forward() that registers itself on the autograd tape
2. Define a custom backward() that computes gradients for the custom op
3. Save tensors for backward via ctx.save_for_backward()

Implementation plan:
1. Add a CustomFunction trait in ft-autograd with forward/backward methods
2. Add a TensorNodeOp::Custom variant to the tensor tape
3. Add session.apply_custom_function() to ft-api that invokes the user's forward, registers the op on the tape, and stores the backward closure
4. During backward traversal, call the user's backward function when encountering Custom nodes
5. Handle save_for_backward by capturing TensorNodeIds in the closure context

Key design decisions:
- The backward closure must receive upstream gradient and return downstream gradients
- Must respect no_grad context (custom forward inside no_grad should not record on tape)
- Must work with the existing deterministic backward scheduler
- Error handling: user backward functions should return Result, not panic

Tests:
- Unit test: custom ReLU equivalent (forward: max(0,x), backward: 1 if x>0 else 0)
- Unit test: custom function with multiple inputs/outputs
- Unit test: custom function inside no_grad context (should not record)
- Unit test: custom function backward produces correct gradients (numerical gradient check)
- E2E test: train a model with a custom layer and verify loss decreases
- Edge case: nested custom functions
- Edge case: custom function that saves tensors for backward","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:16:46.006446323Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T20:15:34.162735830Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T20:15:34.162441333Z","close_reason":"Core functionality already exists: tensor_apply_function() with FunctionCtx (save_for_backward, needs_input_grad) + backward closure. Extensive tests in ft-autograd (lines 17484+) and used internally by ft-api for ~12 ops. The closure-based API is the Rust-idiomatic equivalent of PyTorch's class-based torch.autograd.Function. A trait wrapper could be added later for API sugar but is not blocking.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-2os","title":"Implement slogdet, pinverse, kthvalue, linalg_cross, linalg_vector_norm","description":"Add numerically stable linalg and selection ops: slogdet (sign+log determinant), pinverse (SVD-based pseudoinverse), kthvalue (k-th smallest), linalg_cross (3D cross product), linalg_vector_norm (flexible vector norm). Used in scientific computing, regularization, and model analysis.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-28T14:15:54.437563761Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-29T02:09:46.045687331Z","closed_at":"2026-03-29T02:09:46.045431557Z","close_reason":"slogdet/cross already existed. Implemented pinverse (SVD-based), kthvalue (with index), linalg_vector_norm (L0/L1/L2/Linf). 9 tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankentorch-35e","title":"Update FEATURE_PARITY.md to honestly track unimplemented PyTorch subsystems","description":"FEATURE_PARITY.md currently marks 'Full PyTorch drop-in surface: parity_green' but omits major PyTorch subsystems from the tracking matrix entirely, creating a misleading picture.

What to add:
1. New section: 'Deferred Subsystems (not yet in scope)' with explicit not_started status for: GPU/CUDA backend, Distributed training (DDP/FSDP), JIT/TorchScript/torch.compile, Quantization, Sparse tensors, Mixed precision (AMP), ONNX export, Gradient checkpointing, Custom autograd Functions (torch.autograd.Function).
2. For each: document why it's deferred (e.g., 'CPU-first vertical slice, GPU planned for Phase 3+'), link to any future closure beads.
3. Change 'Full PyTorch drop-in surface: parity_green' to accurately reflect that it covers the CPU eager-mode subset only.
4. Add a 'Parity Scope Declaration' section that explicitly states what IS and IS NOT in scope for the current phase.

This is the single most important documentation fix — without it, the project appears done when it's at ~70% of PyTorch's core API surface.

Tests: N/A (documentation only). Verify by reading the updated file and confirming every major PyTorch subsystem is either tracked as green or explicitly listed as deferred.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:16:28.495902714Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:28:40.000156666Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T04:28:39.999778249Z","close_reason":"Updated FEATURE_PARITY.md with explicit CPU eager-mode scope declaration, deferred subsystem table, truthful full-parity status, and closure beads for missing top-level PyTorch subsystems","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"frankentorch-3cp","title":"Capture committed perf-SLO baseline artifact for G5","description":"Follow-up to frankentorch-vws.\n\nThe perf-SLO runner/gate now exists in ft-conformance, strict cargo gates pass, and we captured a verified single-sample full-profile measurement in artifacts/phase2c/performance/perf_slo_report_full_once.json plus PERF_SLO_SUMMARY_FULL_ONCE.md. The current result shows all five direct spec budgets failing and the three regression budgets remaining baseline_required without a committed baseline.\n\nRemaining work:\n1. Run the full perf-SLO profile with the intended statistical envelope (default 100 iterations, warmups as configured) on a suitable worker/host.\n2. Commit a stable baseline artifact at the canonical perf_slo baseline path for regression comparisons.\n3. Decide whether the single-sample full_once artifact should stay as an exploratory snapshot or be superseded by the committed baseline bundle.\n4. Hand the baseline path/contract to frankentorch-ioo so G5 can call run_perf_slos + check_perf_slos deterministically in CI.\n\nNotes:\n- rch exec validates runtime execution but does not automatically persist generated artifacts back into the local worktree across separate invocations, so baseline capture should use a workflow that intentionally preserves the produced artifact bundle.\n- Direct spec-budget failures from the single-sample full profile are already documented in artifacts/phase2c/performance/PERF_SLO_SUMMARY_FULL_ONCE.md.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T20:53:37.780725984Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T20:53:37.780725984Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankentorch-b6n","title":"Audit ft-data loading path for latent correctness bugs","description":"## Context
The explicit parity backlog is empty, but we still need fresh-eyes subsystem audits to find latent correctness bugs hidden behind current green status. Start with ft-data and trace its batching/shuffle/drop_last behavior through tests and adjacent crate usage.

## Goal
Identify and fix any obvious correctness bug in the ft-data loading path, then verify with focused tests and workspace gates.

## Acceptance
- trace ft-data end-to-end
- fix at least one high-confidence correctness bug if found
- add/adjust tests proving the fix
- verify with rch cargo check/clippy/tests","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-04T01:10:45.588213207Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-04T01:20:40.140473566Z","closed_at":"2026-04-04T01:20:40.140223743Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-b8f","title":"ft-api: fail closed on out-of-range indexed write ops","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-06T21:40:21.732572151Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:18.086308785Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T21:43:18.085991304Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-c3d","title":"Add CUDA/GPU execution backend","description":"FrankenTorch currently exposes `Device::Cuda` tags and device-guard validation, but all real execution remains CPU-only. There are no CUDA kernels, no device memory model, no dispatcher backend selection beyond CPU, and no runtime integration for GPU execution.

Scope for eventual closure:
1. Add concrete GPU storage/device abstractions beyond enum tagging
2. Implement CUDA dispatch keys and backend routing
3. Port core kernels (elementwise, reductions, matmul, indexing) to GPU
4. Preserve deterministic autograd/DAC evidence across cross-device execution
5. Add conformance and benchmark coverage for CPU↔GPU parity

This bead exists so FEATURE_PARITY.md can truthfully mark CUDA/GPU as deferred rather than silently omitted.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:23:50.059185557Z","created_by":"CopperStork","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:23:50.059185557Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankentorch-c7c","title":"Add end-to-end training example and integration test","description":"FrankenTorch has e2e training loop tests buried in ft-optim's test suite, but there is no standalone, documented example showing users how to train a model. This is critical for proving the system works end-to-end and for user onboarding.

Create a comprehensive integration test (in ft-conformance or a new examples/ directory) that:

1. Defines a 2-layer MLP: Linear(784, 128) -> ReLU -> Linear(128, 10)
2. Generates synthetic MNIST-like data using the existing TensorDataset/DataLoader
3. Trains for 5 epochs using Adam optimizer with learning rate scheduling (StepLR or CosineAnnealing)
4. Tracks loss per epoch and asserts it decreases monotonically (with tolerance for noise)
5. Uses no_grad context for evaluation pass
6. Saves model state_dict to file, loads it back, verifies predictions match
7. Uses BatchNorm or LayerNorm in the architecture to exercise normalization paths
8. Logs: epoch number, batch count, loss per epoch, final accuracy estimate, elapsed time

This test exercises the full stack: ft-data (DataLoader) -> ft-nn (modules) -> ft-api (forward/backward) -> ft-autograd (gradient computation) -> ft-optim (parameter update) -> ft-serialize (save/load).

If any component fails to compose correctly, this test catches it.

Tests:
- The example IS the test: it should be a #[test] fn in ft-conformance or similar
- Assert loss decreases over 5 epochs
- Assert save/load round-trip produces identical predictions
- Assert no_grad eval context doesn't corrupt gradient state
- Log detailed per-epoch metrics for debugging","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:01.270413750Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:01.270413750Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankentorch-c7c","title":"Add end-to-end training example and integration test","description":"FrankenTorch has e2e training loop tests buried in ft-optim's test suite, but there is no standalone, documented example showing users how to train a model. This is critical for proving the system works end-to-end and for user onboarding.

Create a comprehensive integration test (in ft-conformance or a new examples/ directory) that:

1. Defines a 2-layer MLP: Linear(784, 128) -> ReLU -> Linear(128, 10)
2. Generates synthetic MNIST-like data using the existing TensorDataset/DataLoader
3. Trains for 5 epochs using Adam optimizer with learning rate scheduling (StepLR or CosineAnnealing)
4. Tracks loss per epoch and asserts it decreases monotonically (with tolerance for noise)
5. Uses no_grad context for evaluation pass
6. Saves model state_dict to file, loads it back, verifies predictions match
7. Uses BatchNorm or LayerNorm in the architecture to exercise normalization paths
8. Logs: epoch number, batch count, loss per epoch, final accuracy estimate, elapsed time

This test exercises the full stack: ft-data (DataLoader) -> ft-nn (modules) -> ft-api (forward/backward) -> ft-autograd (gradient computation) -> ft-optim (parameter update) -> ft-serialize (save/load).

If any component fails to compose correctly, this test catches it.

Tests:
- The example IS the test: it should be a #[test] fn in ft-conformance or similar
- Assert loss decreases over 5 epochs
- Assert save/load round-trip produces identical predictions
- Assert no_grad eval context doesn't corrupt gradient state
- Log detailed per-epoch metrics for debugging","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:01.270413750Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T20:14:50.299054985Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T20:14:50.298796636Z","close_reason":"Added 5 E2E integration tests exercising the full stack: MLP regression with DataLoader/Adam/StepLR, binary classification with cross-entropy, save/load round-trip verification, no_grad eval safety, dataloader epoch shuffle. All pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-c84","title":"Diagnose residual beads integrity-check warning","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-07T01:27:54.222375196Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T01:35:07.976091802Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T01:35:07.975908695Z","close_reason":"Diagnosed residual integrity warning: live DB and JSONL stay in sync; isolated rebuild reproduces the warning; VACUUM yields a clean DB; subsequent normal br write paths reintroduce 'never used' pages, pointing to a beads_rust/frankensqlite writer/page-accounting bug rather than JSONL drift.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankentorch-i7v","title":"Restore ft-optim rustfmt compliance","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-31T03:32:23.707770910Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-31T03:34:06.440692399Z","closed_at":"2026-03-31T03:34:06.440430122Z","close_reason":"Completed: restored ft-optim rustfmt compliance","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankentorch-iey","title":"Add ONNX model export support","description":"ONNX export allows FrankenTorch models to be deployed in any ONNX-compatible runtime (ONNX Runtime, TensorRT, CoreML, etc.). This is important for production deployment and interoperability.

Implementation plan:
1. Create a new crate ft-onnx (or module in ft-serialize) that:
   a. Traces a model forward pass to capture the computation graph
   b. Maps FrankenTorch ops to ONNX operator set (opset 17+)
   c. Serializes the graph + weights to ONNX protobuf format
2. Add session.export_onnx(model, dummy_input, path) to ft-api
3. Map core ops: Linear, Conv2d, ReLU, BatchNorm, LSTM, Softmax, MatMul, Add, etc.
4. Use the onnx-pb crate for protobuf serialization (or manual protobuf encoding)

Tests:
- Unit test: export a Linear model, verify ONNX file is valid protobuf
- Unit test: exported model contains correct op types and shapes
- Integration test (if onnxruntime-rs available): run exported model and compare outputs
- Edge case: export model with dynamic batch size","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:19:10.592448782Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:19:22.990921829Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankentorch-iey","depends_on_id":"frankentorch-2er","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:19:22.990453482Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"frankentorch-igu","title":"Add adversarial/fuzz test coverage for high-risk paths (spec Gate G4)","description":"COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENTORCH_V1.md §5 requires 'adversarial fixtures and fuzz/property suites for high-risk entry points' and §18 defines Gate G4 (adversarial + property tests) as release-blocking.

Current state: ft-serialize has 5 proptest properties. ft-core has none. ft-api, ft-autograd, ft-kernel-cpu have none. The spec's Security Threat Matrix (§16) defines 8 threat categories that need adversarial coverage.

Implementation plan:
1. Add proptest properties for ft-autograd:
   - Random computation graphs maintain gradient correctness (numerical gradient check)
   - Backward on disconnected subgraphs doesn't corrupt other gradients
   - In-place mutation version tracking prevents stale gradient reads
2. Add proptest properties for ft-kernel-cpu:
   - Broadcasting produces correct shapes for random input shapes
   - Reduction ops produce correct results for random input tensors (cross-check against naive impl)
   - Matmul associativity: (A*B)*C matches A*(B*C) within tolerance
3. Add proptest properties for ft-api:
   - Random tensor ops maintain shape consistency (output shape matches PyTorch rules)
   - save_state_dict -> load_state_dict round-trip preserves all values for random state dicts
4. Add adversarial fixture tests for ft-serialize:
   - Fuzzed JSON payloads don't cause panics (catch_unwind + verify Error returned)
   - Fuzzed binary payloads don't cause panics
   - Oversized shape dimensions don't cause OOM (our recent checked_mul fix)
5. Add adversarial tests for ft-nn:
   - NaN/Inf inputs to forward() produce NaN/Inf output (not panic)
   - Zero-sized batch inputs handled gracefully

Tests: The beads ARE the tests. Each property test should use proptest with 256+ cases.

+{"id":"frankentorch-igu","title":"Add adversarial/fuzz test coverage for high-risk paths (spec Gate G4)","description":"COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENTORCH_V1.md §5 requires 'adversarial fixtures and fuzz/property suites for high-risk entry points' and §18 defines Gate G4 (adversarial + property tests) as release-blocking.

2. Add proptest properties for ft-kernel-cpu:

4. Add adversarial fixture tests for ft-serialize:
   - Fuzzed JSON payloads don't cause panics (catch_unwind + verify Error returned)
   - Fuzzed binary payloads don't cause panics
   - Oversized shape dimensions don't cause OOM (our recent checked_mul fix)
5. Add adversarial tests for ft-nn:
   - NaN/Inf inputs to forward() produce NaN/Inf output (not panic)
   - Zero-sized batch inputs handled gracefully

 {"id":"frankentorch-iha","title":"Add mixed precision support (torch.amp equivalent)","description":"torch.cuda.amp (Automatic Mixed Precision) is used in virtually every modern training pipeline. Even on CPU, F16/BF16 mixed precision can be useful for inference and for testing AMP workflows before moving to GPU.

Since FrankenTorch already supports F16 and BF16 dtypes in ft-core, the infrastructure exists — what's missing is the autocast and grad scaling machinery.

Implementation plan:
1. Add an AutocastContext to ft-api that tracks whether autocast is active and the target dtype
2. Add session.autocast_enter(dtype) / session.autocast_exit() (matching no_grad pattern)
3. Add session.with_autocast(dtype, closure) panic-safe wrapper
4. When autocast is active: matmul, linear, conv operations auto-downcast inputs to the target dtype (F16/BF16), then upcast output back to F32
5. Add a GradScaler struct in ft-optim that:
   a. Scales loss by a factor before backward (prevents F16 underflow)
   b. Unscales gradients before optimizer step
   c. Checks for inf/nan in gradients, skipping the update if found
   d. Adjusts scale factor dynamically based on overflow frequency

Key: The autocast policy (which ops to cast) should match PyTorch's known-safe list — matmul, linear, conv are safe; softmax, layernorm, loss functions should stay in F32.

Tests:
- Unit test: autocast context enters/exits correctly, respects nesting
- Unit test: matmul inside autocast uses F16, outside uses F32
- Unit test: GradScaler scales loss and unscales gradients
- Unit test: GradScaler skips update on inf gradients
- E2E test: train a linear model with autocast + GradScaler, verify convergence
- Panic-safety: with_autocast restores state on unwind","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:18:09.970716552Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:18:09.970716552Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"frankentorch-ioo","title":"Add CI pipeline with G1-G5 quality gates from spec","description":"COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENTORCH_V1.md §18 defines 5 CI gates that are release-blocking. NONE of them exist:

| Gate | Name | Blocking | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| G1 | format + lint | yes | lint report |
| G2 | unit + integration | yes | junit report |
| G3 | differential + gradient checks | yes | parity report + grad report |
| G4 | adversarial + property tests | yes | counterexample corpus |
| G5 | benchmark regression | yes | baseline delta report |

Implementation:
1. Create .github/workflows/ci.yml with 5 jobs mapping to G1-G5
2. G1: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -- -D warnings
3. G2: cargo test --workspace --all-features (with junit output via cargo-nextest or similar)
4. G3: cargo test -p ft-conformance (differential fixtures), cargo test -p ft-autograd (gradient checks)
5. G4: cargo test --workspace --all-features (proptest runs as part of normal test suite)
6. G5: cargo run -p ft-conformance --bin run_perf_slos (depends on frankentorch-vws being done first)

Considerations:
- GitHub Actions runners may be slow for full test suite; consider splitting into parallel jobs
- G5 (benchmark regression) needs baseline artifacts committed to repo for comparison
- Need to use nightly toolchain (rust-toolchain.toml already specifies this)

Tests:
- The CI pipeline IS the test — verify it by pushing a PR and checking all 5 gates pass
- Ensure the pipeline also runs on the 'main' branch (not just PRs)
- Add a badge to README.md showing CI status","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:38.761474655Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:19:22.901208090Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankentorch-ioo","depends_on_id":"frankentorch-vws","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:19:22.900747037Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"frankentorch-jhi","title":"Make Optimizer::set_momentum return Result instead of silent no-op","description":"## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) found that `Optimizer::set_momentum(&mut self, _momentum: f64)` in ft-optim/src/lib.rs:111 has an empty body `{}`. This is the ONLY empty function body in the entire codebase.

The paired `get_momentum()` default returns `None`, which is correct. But `set_momentum()` silently discards the value with no feedback — a caller setting momentum on an Adagrad optimizer (which does not support momentum) gets no error and no indication that their intent was ignored.

## Problem
Silent data loss violates the principle of least surprise. In PyTorch, calling an unsupported setter is either a no-op with a warning or raises an error depending on the optimizer. FrankenTorch should be at least as explicit.

## Plan
1. Change signature to `fn set_momentum(&mut self, _momentum: f64) -> Result<(), AutogradError>`
2. Default implementation returns `Err(optimizer_hparam_error("this optimizer does not support momentum"))`
3. SGD (and any other momentum-supporting optimizer) overrides to actually set the value and return `Ok(())`
4. Update all callers to handle the Result
5. Add a test verifying that non-momentum optimizers reject set_momentum

## Acceptance Criteria
- `set_momentum` on Adagrad/RMSprop/RAdam returns descriptive Err
- `set_momentum` on SGD succeeds
- All existing optimizer tests pass
- Zero empty function bodies remain in the codebase

## Dependencies
None — this is an independent API change within ft-optim.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-02T23:22:34.711976324Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-02T23:45:29.961692699Z","closed_at":"2026-04-02T23:45:29.961433909Z","close_reason":"Optimizer::set_momentum now returns an explicit unsupported-feature error by default, SGD implements Result-based momentum updates, OneCycleLR handles failures visibly, and ft-optim tests plus workspace check/clippy passed via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"frankentorch-plj","title":"Add gradient checkpointing (torch.utils.checkpoint equivalent)","description":"torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint() allows trading compute for memory by not saving intermediate activations during forward, then recomputing them during backward. This is essential for training large models that don't fit in memory.

Implementation plan:
1. Add a session.checkpoint(function, inputs) method that:
   a. Runs the function in no_grad context (saves no intermediates)
   b. Records a special CheckpointedSegment node on the autograd tape
   c. During backward: re-runs the function WITH grad enabled to recompute activations, then computes gradients normally
2. The key invariant: gradients must be identical whether checkpoint is used or not (just slower with lower memory)

Design considerations:
- Must work with the existing TensorTape backward scheduler
- Must properly handle nested checkpointing
- Must respect the DAC contract (deterministic replay must still work)
- Re-execution during backward must produce identical activations (deterministic PRNG state needs saving)

Tests:
- Unit test: simple function with checkpoint vs without — gradients must match exactly
- Unit test: nested checkpointing (checkpoint inside checkpoint)
- Unit test: checkpoint respects manual_seed for reproducibility
- E2E test: train a deep network with and without checkpointing — final loss should be identical
- Memory test: verify peak memory is lower with checkpointing (if measurable)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:52.757885399Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:52.757885399Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Runs function in no_grad (no intermediates saved), registers backward node via tensor_apply_function that re-executes the function with grad enabled during backward. 3 tests: correct forward output, gradient equivalence with non-checkpointed, multiple inputs.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankentorch-qcs","title":"Add integration test verifying zero panic/todo/unimplemented in production code","description":"## Context
The stub/placeholder audit (2026-04-02) was a manual multi-agent process. To prevent regressions, we need an automated gate that catches newly-introduced stubs, placeholders, and panic-inducing macros in production code.

All prerequisite beads completed: dwy (storage panic), jhi (set_momentum), aqm (DType exhaustiveness), aca (unreachable messages), tk5 (module error enrichment).

## Goal
Create a source-scanning integration test in ft-conformance that fails if prohibited patterns appear in production Rust source code.

## Prohibited patterns (MUST cause test failure in production code)
1. `todo!()` macro
2. `unimplemented!()` macro
3. `panic!(` — bare panic calls (NOT assert!/debug_assert! which are different macros)

## Allowed patterns (MUST NOT cause false positives)
1. Any pattern inside `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` blocks — tests can panic freely
2. `unreachable!("...")\ — with a message string (legitimate defensive assertions)
3. `assert!()`, `assert_eq!()`, `debug_assert!()` — NOT panic calls
4. String literals that happen to contain prohibited words (e.g., test case names like "panic!")
5. Doc comments that use words like "placeholder" in their technical meaning (e.g., nn.Identity's "Useful as a placeholder in Sequential containers")

## Known legitimate use of 'placeholder' word
The Identity module doc in ft-nn/src/lib.rs says: "Useful as a placeholder in Sequential containers or for skip connections". This is a technical description of nn.Identity's PURPOSE, not a code-incompleteness marker. The stub-marker test must NOT flag doc-comment uses of these words when they appear inside `///` or `//!` doc comments.

## Implementation approach: scan-up-to-cfg-test
Use the simple approach: for each .rs file, find the line number of `#[cfg(test)]` and only scan lines BEFORE that marker. This is correct for this codebase because:
- Every crate has exactly one `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block at the bottom
- No test code appears above this marker
- Binary crates (ft-conformance/src/bin/*.rs) have NO test blocks — scan the entire file

For files WITHOUT a #[cfg(test)] marker, scan the entire file.

## Test file location
`crates/ft-conformance/tests/stub_audit.rs` — standalone integration test.

## Test functions to implement (5 total)

### 1. test_no_todo_in_production
Scan all .rs files in crates/ (recursively). For each file, scan only production lines (before #[cfg(test)]). Assert zero lines matching the regex `\btodo!\(`. Print violations as `VIOLATION: {path}:{line_num}: {trimmed_line}`.

### 2. test_no_unimplemented_in_production
Same approach for `\bunimplemented!\(`.

### 3. test_no_bare_panic_in_production
Scan production lines for `panic!(\(`. EXCLUDE lines that:
- Are inside string literals (line starts with `"` after trimming, or contains `=>" panic")
- Contain "panic!" as a string literal (e.g., in test fixture data like smoke.rs line 156)
To avoid complex string-literal parsing, use a simple heuristic: skip lines where `panic!` appears after a `"` character that is not preceded by a backslash.

### 4. test_no_bare_unreachable_without_message
Scan production lines for bare `unreachable!()` (with empty parens — no message). Assert zero matches. Lines with `unreachable!("...")\ are fine.

### 5. test_no_stub_markers_in_production_comments
Scan production lines for TODO, FIXME, HACK, STUB (case-insensitive) in comments. A "comment" line is one where the pattern appears after `//` (line comment) or inside `/* */` (block comment). EXCLUDE matches inside doc comments (`///` and `//!`) since those may legitimately use words like "placeholder" in technical descriptions.

## Logging requirements
Each test MUST use `eprintln!` for structured output:
- On start: `[stub_audit] Scanning {N} files for {pattern_name}...`
- Per violation: `[stub_audit] VIOLATION {path}:{line}: {pattern}: {content}`
- On finish: `[stub_audit] {pattern_name}: {violations} violations in {files} files ({prod_lines} production lines)`

## Testing the tests themselves
After implementing, verify:
1. All 5 tests pass on current codebase: `rch exec -- cargo test -p ft-conformance --test stub_audit -- --nocapture`
2. Inject `todo!()` into ft-core/src/lib.rs line 2, run tests, confirm test_no_todo_in_production FAILS
3. Remove the injected todo!(), confirm tests pass again
4. Verify the Identity module "placeholder" doc comment does NOT trigger test_no_stub_markers

## Acceptance Criteria
- 5 test functions in stub_audit.rs, all passing
- Each test has structured logging
- Zero false positives on current codebase
- Confirmed that injecting prohibited patterns causes test failure
- Clippy clean on the new test file

## Dependencies
- Blocked by: frankentorch-dwy (CLOSED), frankentorch-aca (CLOSED) — both resolved","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-02T23:23:46.486851149Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T02:11:44.707272087Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T02:11:44.707100282Z","close_reason":"Added ft-conformance regression gate that scans production Rust sources for todo!/unimplemented!/panic! while skipping test-only code, and verified the new smoke test plus workspace check/clippy via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankentorch-qcs","depends_on_id":"frankentorch-aca","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-02T23:24:13.807546637Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"frankentorch-qcs","depends_on_id":"frankentorch-dwy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-02T23:24:13.730286250Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"frankentorch-vub","title":"Eliminate ft-conformance dead_code warnings blocking clippy","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-27T05:28:12.142290137Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-27T05:31:52.217395929Z","closed_at":"2026-03-27T05:31:52.217160484Z","close_reason":"Validated fixture metadata and removed ft-conformance dead_code warnings","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"frankentorch-vws","title":"Validate performance SLOs from spec against actual measurements","description":"COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENTORCH_V1.md §17 defines 8 performance budgets that have NEVER been measured:

| Path | Workload | Budget |
|---|---|---|
| elementwise forward | 100M elements | p95 <= 200 ms |
| backward pass | representative autograd graphs | p95 <= 1.35x forward baseline |
| matmul core | medium dense matrices | p95 <= 240 ms |
| optimizer step | 10M params Adam | p95 <= 130 ms |
| checkpoint save/load | 2 GB model state | throughput >= 350 MB/s |
| memory footprint | training step profile | peak RSS regression <= +8% |
| allocation churn | backward-heavy trace | alloc count regression <= +10% |
| tail stability | benchmark families | p99 regression <= +7% |

The spec's M4 milestone (Hardening) requires performance gates to pass. Currently we have exactly one benchmark (scalar DAC microbench, p95=4698ns) which doesn't cover any of the 8 SLOs.

Implementation plan:
1. Create a benchmark binary in ft-conformance (or a new crate) that runs each of the 8 workloads
2. For each: run 100 iterations, compute p50/p95/p99, compare against the budget
3. Emit structured JSON results to artifacts/phase2c/performance/
4. Create a gate script that reads the JSON and fails if any SLO is exceeded
5. Document whether FrankenTorch meets, exceeds, or fails each budget

Key consideration: We're CPU-only and pure Rust with no BLAS — we may not meet matmul or elementwise budgets for 100M elements. That's OK — the goal is to MEASURE and know where we stand, then document realistic budgets.

Tests:
- Benchmark binary should be runnable via: rch exec -- cargo run -p ft-conformance --bin run_perf_slos
- JSON output should be machine-readable and diffable
- Gate script should exit 0 (pass) or 1 (fail) with human-readable report","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:24.426519762Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T04:17:24.426519762Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
